question.en: Which of the following statements best distinguishes an Entity from a Value Object in DDD?

text.en: Entities live in the database; Value Objects live only in memory.
Entities are defined by an identity that persists over time; Value Objects are defined entirely by their attributes and have no independent identity.
Entities are mutable and Value Objects are mutable too, but Value Objects must inherit from a base class.
Entities can have methods; Value Objects can only have data fields.

explanation.en: An Entity has a thread of identity that runs through time — two entities with identical attributes are still different if they have different IDs (e.g. two Orders, two Customers). A Value Object has no identity; it is equal to any other Value Object with the same attributes (e.g. two Money(100, 'USD') instances are interchangeable). Value Objects are also immutable, while Entities are mutable.

question.en: A Bounded Context must be deployed as exactly one microservice.

text.en: True
False

explanation.en: False. A Bounded Context is a modeling boundary (linguistic and conceptual) — it defines where a particular domain model and ubiquitous language apply. A microservice is a deployment boundary. A single Bounded Context can be deployed as a monolith, as one service, or as several services. Equating one-to-one with microservices is a common mistake that leads to over-fragmentation and tight coupling between tiny services.

question.en: What is the role of an Aggregate Root?

text.en: It is the database table that stores the aggregate's data.
It is the only object inside an aggregate that external code is allowed to access, and it is responsible for enforcing the aggregate's invariants.
It is a parent class that every entity inside the aggregate must extend.
It is the first event published by the aggregate when it is created.

explanation.en: The Aggregate Root is the single entry point to an aggregate. External code can only modify objects inside the aggregate by going through the root, which is responsible for enforcing the aggregate's invariants (e.g. 'an order with more than 50 items cannot be confirmed'). This protects the consistency of the cluster and makes the root the transactional boundary.

question.en: Which of the following is a classic Value Object?

text.en: Customer (with a unique CustomerId)
Order (tracked by an OrderId across its state transitions)
Money (an amount combined with a currency code, e.g. 100 USD)
Shopping Cart (which persists for a logged-in user across sessions)

explanation.en: Money is the canonical Value Object: it is defined entirely by its attributes (amount + currency), two Money(100, 'USD') instances are equal and interchangeable, and it is immutable — to apply a discount you replace the value with a new one, you do not mutate the original. Customer, Order, and Shopping Cart all have identities that persist over time and are therefore Entities, not Value Objects.

question.en: Ubiquitous Language is a single company-wide glossary that every team uses for every term.

text.en: True
False

explanation.en: False. Ubiquitous Language is strictly scoped to a single Bounded Context. The same real-world concept can — and often should — have different names and different meanings in different contexts (e.g. Product in Catalog vs. Product in Inventory vs. Product in Shipping). Trying to create one enterprise-wide canonical language is the well-known 'God Ubiquitous Language' anti-pattern: it collapses under contradictory meanings and produces bloated, anemic shared models.

question.en: When is it appropriate to introduce a Domain Service rather than putting the logic on an Entity or Value Object?

text.en: Whenever the logic involves a database query.
Whenever the team wants the code to look more 'DDD' by adding a service layer.
When the logic is significant domain behavior that spans multiple aggregates and cannot naturally belong to any single Entity or Value Object.
Whenever the logic needs to send an HTTP request to an external system.

explanation.en: A Domain Service is for stateless domain logic (a real business decision) that does not naturally fit on a single Entity or Value Object — typically because it requires data from two or more aggregates to compute its result. Pricing across cart + customer tier + promotions is a canonical example. Logic that involves databases, HTTP, or other infrastructure orchestration belongs in an Application Service, not a Domain Service.

question.en: Which of the following is correctly modeled as a Domain Event?

text.en: PlaceOrder
OrderProcessing
OrderPlaced
OrderRepository

explanation.en: Domain Events are immutable facts about something that has already happened, named in the past tense — OrderPlaced, PaymentReceived, ShipmentDispatched. 'PlaceOrder' is a command (a request to do something, imperative tense). 'OrderProcessing' is a state, not a fact. 'OrderRepository' is a tactical pattern, not an event.
